analysis:
• Markets price near-zero chance (1-2%) of ceasefire or conflict resolution by end of March, with military action through March 31 at 95%. The earliest meaningful inflection point is mid-April (20-23%), suggesting traders see at best a 1-in-5 chance that the reported Pakistan-channel talks or Trump's 15-point plan produce a deal within three weeks. The heaviest volume sits on the April 30 ceasefire contract ($6.2M, 39%), marking it as the consensus "earliest plausible" window.

• Iran rejected the US 15-point proposal on March 25 as "maximalist and unreasonable" and issued a counterproposal demanding reparations, sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz, and a halt to targeted killings. Despite the rhetoric, mediators (Egypt, Pakistan) are reportedly pushing for in-person talks as early as this weekend — a development not yet fully priced in, given April 7 ceasefire odds remain at just 6-11%.

• Trump extended the pause on Iranian energy infrastructure strikes by 10 days (to ~April 6) on March 26, framing it as a goodwill gesture while claiming talks are going "very well." This is the key near-term catalyst: if the pause lapses without progress, expect April ceasefire contracts to compress sharply. The pause extension likely explains why April 30 odds have held near 39% rather than declining.

• The conflict's humanitarian and economic toll is escalating — 1,900+ killed in Iran, Strait of Hormuz effectively shuttered, and US service members injured in a March 27 attack on a Saudi air base. Regional contagion risk (Lebanon: 1,142 killed since March 2; Saudi Arabia: Polymarket prices 17% chance of Saudi military involvement) adds complexity to any negotiated settlement and supports the longer-dated contracts.

• December 31 ceasefire probability at 76% (US-Iran) and 84% (Iran-Israel/US) implies markets remain confident the conflict ends this year but not quickly. The ~15-point gap between the two contracts suggests traders see a US-Iran bilateral deal as harder to close than a broader conflict wind-down — possibly reflecting Iran's refusal to negotiate directly with Washington.

• Outlook: The next 10 days are pivotal. Watch for (1) whether Pakistan-mediated talks materialize this weekend, (2) the April 6 energy-strike pause deadline, and (3) any shift in Iran's posture on direct negotiations. A confirmed meeting would likely push April 15 odds above 30%; a collapsed pause would crater them below 15%.

Sources:
